title: COCO-LM: Correcting and Contrasting Text Sequences for Language Model Pretraining

structured.summary: COCO-LM proposes a novel pretraining framework that jointly performs corrective language modeling and sequence contrastive learning to improve pretrained language models' efficiency, representation quality, and generative capabilities. It surpasses previous methods like ELECTRA and RoBERTa on standard benchmarks while enabling prompt-based learning due to preserved language modeling capacity.

strengths.point: Innovative combination of corrective language modeling and sequence contrastive learning
strengths.evidence: The main model corrects corrupted tokens generated by an auxiliary MLM while contrasting corrupted sequences with cropped originals to improve token-level accuracy and sequence embedding quality.

strengths.point: Improved efficiency and representation quality over ELECTRA
strengths.evidence: COCO-LM matches or outperforms ELECTRA with about 50-60% of the pretraining GPU hours and achieves better alignment and uniformity in sequence embeddings.

strengths.point: Supports prompt-based fine-tuning by retaining language modeling capability
strengths.evidence: Unlike ELECTRA, COCO-LM's corrective LM head enables generative prompt-based learning, demonstrated by 1+% accuracy gains on few-shot MNLI.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results and ablations demonstrating effectiveness
strengths.evidence: Outperforms state-of-the-art pretrained models on GLUE and SQuAD; ablation studies confirm the importance of each component including stop-gradient, auxiliary model design, and multi-task CLM.

weaknesses.point: Limited comparison to recent contrastive learning methods beyond ELECTRA
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis relative to methods like SimCSE or other contrastive sentence embedding approaches is presented, leaving unclear whether gains stem from CLM, SCL, or their combination.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient exploration of key hyperparameters and training dynamics
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation on cropping ratio in SCL, no empirical analysis of stop-gradient impact, and unclear joint training schedule for auxiliary and main models.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation restricted to English and standard benchmarks
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments only on English datasets (GLUE, SQuAD) without testing generalization to other languages, noisy domains, or out-of-distribution data.

weaknesses.point: Lack of qualitative analyses and failure case discussions
weaknesses.evidence: No human evaluation or error analysis explaining what corrective LM learns or scenarios where COCO-LM underperforms.

weaknesses.point: Prompt-based evaluation limited in scope and detail
weaknesses.evidence: Few-shot experiments confined to MNLI with minimal description of prompt templates, verbalizers, and hyperparameters.

weaknesses.point: Computational cost reporting lacks sufficient detail
weaknesses.evidence: GPU hours reported without batch size, sequence length, or hardware specifics, hindering reproducibility and fair comparison.

weaknesses.point: Comparisons sometimes confound model size and pretraining data scale
weaknesses.evidence: Large++ variants trained on 10x more data than base models, but baselines are not uniformly scaled, making attribution of gains ambiguous.

questions.question: How sensitive is COCO-LM’s performance to the cropping ratio used in sequence contrastive learning?
questions.motivation: Understanding this impacts robustness and applicability across datasets with varying sequence lengths.

questions.question: What is the empirical effect of removing stop-gradient in the multi-task corrective LM setup?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this would validate the claimed training stability benefits and guide future model designs.

questions.question: How does COCO-LM perform on non-English or noisy domain corpora?
questions.motivation: Testing generalizability is critical for deployment in multilingual and real-world settings.

questions.question: Can the authors provide more details or ablation on the joint training schedule between auxiliary and main models?
questions.motivation: This would improve reproducibility and understanding of training dynamics.

questions.question: How does COCO-LM compare to recent contrastive learning methods like SimCSE when integrated or evaluated similarly?
questions.motivation: This would clarify the unique contribution of the proposed SCL component.

critique_points.point_001: Limited comparison to recent contrastive learning methods beyond ELECTRA. No analysis relative to methods like SimCSE or other contrastive sentence embedding approaches is presented, leaving unclear whether gains stem from CLM, SCL, or their combination
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient exploration of key hyperparameters and training dynamics. No ablation on cropping ratio in SCL, no empirical analysis of stop-gradient impact, and unclear joint training schedule for auxiliary and main models
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation restricted to English and standard benchmarks. Experiments only on English datasets (GLUE, SQuAD) without testing generalization to other languages, noisy domains, or out-of-distribution data
critique_points.point_004: Lack of qualitative analyses and failure case discussions. No human evaluation or error analysis explaining what corrective LM learns or scenarios where COCO-LM underperforms
critique_points.point_005: Prompt-based evaluation limited in scope and detail. Few-shot experiments confined to MNLI with minimal description of prompt templates, verbalizers, and hyperparameters
critique_points.point_006: Computational cost reporting lacks sufficient detail. GPU hours reported without batch size, sequence length, or hardware specifics, hindering reproducibility and fair comparison
critique_points.point_007: Comparisons sometimes confound model size and pretraining data scale. Large++ variants trained on 10x more data than base models, but baselines are not uniformly scaled, making attribution of gains ambiguous
